title: Aspidium parasiticum
intro: Aspidium parasiticum, also known as the Indian feather fern or Aspidium, is a species of fern that belongs to the family Dryopteridaceae. The plant has been traditionally used in medicine for its various health benefits, including its anti-inflammatory, antimicrobial, and antioxidant properties, which are said to aid in the treatment of fever, cough, and respiratory issues. The bioactive constituents of Aspidium parasiticum include flavonoids, alkaloids, and saponins, which contribute to its medicinal properties. In traditional Ayurvedic medicine, Aspidium parasiticum is used to prepare various herbal preparations, including decoctions, infusions, and powdered extracts, which are used to treat a range of ailments, including respiratory infections and skin conditions.
taxonomy: Aspidium parasiticum is a type of fern, but it is not a currently recognized species. Instead, it is often classified as a synonym of the species Thelypteris thelypterioides, which belongs to the genus Thelypteris. The genus Thelypteris is a part of the family Thelypteridaceae, which is a family of ferns commonly known as the marsh ferns or the wood ferns. The family Thelypteridaceae is a part of the order Polypodiales, which is a large and diverse order of ferns. The order Polypodiales is part of the class Polypodiopsida, which is a class of vascular plants that also includes horsetails and quillworts.
